topic: An adult child moves back in with their parents
raw_output:
[story 1]

The key felt impossibly heavy in my palm, a cold, metallic weight pressing into my skin. I stood on the threshold of the front door, my breath catching in my throat like shattered glass. This wasn't a homecoming; it was an ambush. The house smelled overwhelmingly of lavender and old paper, a scent that used to be comforting but now just seemed suffocating. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to stretch and coalesce into something watchful. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the doorframe for support, my knuckles white against the painted wood. I took a slow, shallow breath, trying to anchor myself, but the silence within the house was loud, filled with unspoken expectations that clawed at my composure. I just needed to find a corner, a place where I wouldn't have to look at anyone.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this?" Mom’s voice was too bright, too cheerful, slicing through the quiet of the living room. I nodded, a jerky, involuntary movement. My eyes darted around the room, cataloging exits, assessing threats I couldn't see. The furniture seemed to lean in, the familiar patterns on the rug suddenly intricate and menacing. I kept my posture rigid, every muscle coiled tight beneath my borrowed sweater. When Dad offered a warm, paternal smile, it didn't reach his eyes; they seemed distant, assessing. A knot formed deep in my stomach, a cold, hard lump that made my chest feel constricted, as if breathing required a conscious, painful effort. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the nearest wall, but my feet felt cemented to the polished floor.

[story 3]

I dropped my suitcase with a thud that echoed unnervingly in the entryway. The sheer volume of familiar objects—the framed photographs, the stacks of well-worn books—felt like an invasion. My gaze swept over the familiar rooms, and a wave of intense nausea washed over me. Each creak of the floorboards sounded like a footstep approaching from behind. I tried to make myself small, to flatten my presence against the wall, but my limbs felt clumsy and unresponsive. When my mother offered a hug, the pressure of her embrace felt crushing, suffocating. I managed a thin, strained smile, my jaw aching from the effort of maintaining a semblance of normalcy while every instinct screamed at me to run back out into the open air.
